title: Opinion: Bangladesh's Digital Future Cannot Wait for Perfect Conditions
summary: The government's AI strategy is ambitious but the window for early-mover advantage in South Asia is closing fast. We cannot afford gradualism.
content:
The government's newly announced National AI Strategy is a serious document. It is ambitious, it is costed, and it correctly identifies the sectors where artificial intelligence can deliver transformational gains for Bangladesh's development trajectory. But reading it carefully, one cannot shake the feeling that it is still calibrated for a world that is moving more slowly than the one we actually inhabit.

The strategy envisions a five-year rollout. Five years. In the current pace of AI development, that is not a planning horizon — it is an eternity. The models that will be deployed in Bangladesh's district hospitals in 2031 under this strategy's timeline will bear as much resemblance to today's cutting-edge diagnostic AI as a 2021 smartphone bears to a 2026 one. The gap, in other words, will be enormous — and Bangladesh risks building infrastructure for a technology generation that has already been superseded.

The urgency argument is not about chasing trends. It is about the compounding nature of capability gaps. Countries that develop AI literacy, regulatory frameworks, and deployment experience early will have structural advantages that are extremely difficult to overcome later. India understood this. Vietnam understood this. The question is whether Bangladesh's policymakers are willing to accept the messiness and risk of moving faster, or whether they will continue to prefer the comfort of comprehensive plans executed at a pace that feels manageable but accomplishes less than the moment demands.

None of this is to say the strategy should be abandoned. It should be accelerated. The pilot programs should launch this quarter, not next year. The AI Research Institute should open its doors with temporary facilities before its permanent campus is built. The regulatory sandbox should be operational before the regulations themselves are finalised. Bangladesh has consistently surprised the world with what it can accomplish when urgency is matched with organisation. This is a moment that calls for exactly that combination.

title: Opinion: The UN Veto Is Not a Bug in the International System — It Is the System
summary: Every time the Security Council fails to act on a humanitarian crisis, commentators call for veto reform. They are missing the deeper truth about how global power actually works.
content:
The United States has again vetoed a UN Security Council resolution demanding a ceasefire in Gaza. The condemnations have followed with the predictability of a script written decades ago. Reform the veto, critics cry. Abolish the permanent five structure. Make the Council representative of the world as it exists today, not as it was arranged in 1945.

These are legitimate arguments. They are also, in a meaningful sense, beside the point — because the veto power is not a flaw in the design of the international order. It is the design. And understanding why tells us something important about what international institutions can and cannot do.

The United Nations was founded on a paradox. Its architects wanted an institution capable of maintaining international peace and security, but they also knew that no such institution could function without the participation of the world's great powers. And great powers, by definition, will not join institutions that can compel them to act against their fundamental interests. The veto was the price of American, Soviet, British, French, and Chinese membership. Without it, there would have been no UN at all.

This does not mean the current arrangement is just. The people of Gaza, or of Darfur, or of any other territory where Security Council paralysis has enabled mass atrocities, have every moral reason to find the veto system abhorrent. Their grievance is entirely legitimate.

But the path to a more responsive international order runs not through procedural reform of the Council — which the permanent members will never permit — but through the painstaking work of building international norms, regional institutions, and coalitions of states willing to act when the Council cannot. That work is slower, messier, and less satisfying than demanding structural reform. It is also the only work that is actually available to us.
